ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: To Cyprian, Deacon.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to Cyprian, deacon and rector of the patrimony of Sicily.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Concerning the Manicheans who are on our possessions I have frequently admonished thy Love to press them with the utmost diligence, and recall them to the Catholic faith.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: If, then, the time requires it, make enquiries in person, or, if other business does not allow this, through others.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Further, it has come to my ears that there are Hebrews on our possessions who will not by any means be converted to God.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But it seems to me that thou shouldest send letters through all our possessions on which these Hebrews are known to be, promising them particularly from me that whosoever of them shall have been converted to our true Lord God Jesus Christ shall have the burdens of his holding lightened.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And this I wish to have done in such sort that, if one has a payment to make of one solidus, a third should be remitted him; if of three or four, that one solidus should be remitted; if of any more, the remission should still be made in the same proportion, or at any rate according as thy Love sees fit, so that one who is converted may have some relief of his burden, and the Church may not be put to heavy expense.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Nor shall we do this unprofitably, if by lightening the burdens of their payments we bring them to the grace of Christ, since, though they themselves came with little faith, yet those who may be born of them will now be baptized with more faith: thus we gain either them or their children.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And whatever amount of payment we let them off for the sake of Christ is nothing serious.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Furthermore, some time ago, when John the deacon came, thy Love wrote something to me, the whole of which I read at the time, but let many days intervene before replying; and then, after such delay, replied to all particulars as I recollected them.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But now I think that one point escaped my memory, and suspect that I gave no reply about it.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For thou hadst written that loans were being advanced to peasants (rusticis) through certain undertakers for their debt, lest in borrowing from others they should be burdened either by exactions or by the prices of things.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: This particular was to me most acceptable; and, if indeed I have already written about it, observe what I wrote.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But if, as I suspect, I gave in my reply no definite direction on the subject, thou must not hesitate to advance money for the advantage of the peasants, since the ecclesiastical property will not thus be wasted, and out of it the peasants will derive advantage.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, if there are other things which thou considerest to be advantageous, thou must carry them out without any hesitation.
